figures.caption: Table 7: Example export types produced by the same engine.
Table 16: Operational contract for the generation-time inner loop.

Figure 12: Supplementary audit views for the Eval1 Test Set external comparison. Positive values in the normalized subplot indicate improvement after accounting for metric direction; the per-angle subplots show that the same model ordering persists across the angle breakdown.

Figure 11: Supplementary audit views for the SpatialEdit-Bench external comparison. Positive values in the normalized subplot indicate improvement after accounting for whether a metric is higher-is-better or lower-is-better; the per-angle PSNR and DINO subplots show that the same ordering remains visible across angle bins.
Figure 3: External comparison on SpatialEdit-Bench. The final Ours +DualGate system is best on PSNR, SSIM, LPIPS, CLIP-I, and DINO.
Table 15: Priority roadmap for future engine releases.
Table 10: External comparison on SpatialEdit-Bench (488 pairs) using Base, Public LoRA, and the final Ours +DualGate configuration.
Table 8: Ablation path from early rotation probes to the reader-facing four-stage chain used in the results.

Figure 1: Overall goal-driven-loop-agent workflow engine. A visual data request is converted into an artifact graph, reviewed by VLM/CV signals, routed through explicit verdicts, and validated by downstream probes rather than silently merged into training data.
Table 1: Supported output modalities in the DataEvolver artifact schema.
Table 2: Representative transformation families and their image/video export modes.
Figure 8: Four-stage ablation chain for the goal-driven-loop-agent data engine. The final Ours +DualGate variant is best on both traditional and VIE metrics.

Figure 9: Per-angle diagnostics for the four-stage ablation chain. Improvements are visible in the overall averages and in several angle-specific subgroups, although the magnitude varies by angle. Because SpatialEdit-Bench uses an eight-slot evaluation protocol, the per-angle diagnostic includes a front-equivalent full-rotation evaluation bin, whereas our constructed training split uses only the seven non-front target views. In the appendix audits, this front-equivalent evaluation slot is denoted as 360◦.

Figure 6: Representative qualitative comparison on an out-of-domain example from the SpatialEditBench rotate subset. Ours +DualGate shows better viewpoint accuracy and object consistency than Base and Public LoRA, while the two external baselines exhibit visible artifacts and less stable structure.

Figure 7: A second out-of-domain qualitative comparison from the SpatialEdit-Bench rotate subset. The same trend holds on a different object category: Ours +DualGate remains closer to the target rotation sequence and ground-truth appearance, whereas Base and Public LoRA again show strong artifact and consistency failures.

Figure 2: Dual-loop self-evolving visual data construction under goal-driven loop agents. The inner loop corrects generation-time artifacts, while the outer loop converts downstream evaluation into the next data-construction decision.

Figure 10: Qualitative effect of the full closed-loop variant within the same model family. Compared with Ours-Base, Ours +DualGate produces a more coherent side/back transition and more stable object structure across neighboring viewpoints.
Figure 4: External comparison on the Eval1 Test Set. The final Ours +DualGate system again leads Base and Public LoRA on all five metrics.

Figure 5: Representative qualitative comparison on the held-out in-domain evaluation set constructed from the same rendering family as the training data. Ours +DualGate more closely follows the requested azimuth sequence and preserves object appearance better than Base and Public LoRA, both of which show visible artifacts in multiple views.
